name: Castillo-Palacio de Castronuevo — circular por los Parajes de La Mora
region_intro: Hiking around Crespos offers a network of trails characterized by gentle elevation changes and open countryside. The region features historical paths connecting small villages and agricultural landscapes. These routes provide opportunities for easy hikes Crespos, exploring the local terrain without significant climbs. The area is defined by its rural character, with paths often leading through fields and past historical structures. Best hiking trails around Crespos The most popular hiking route is Castillo-Palacio de Castronuevo — circular por los Parajes de La Mora, a 9.3 miles (15.0 km) trail that takes 3 hours 53 minutes to complete. This moderate circular route explores the rural surroundings and historical sites. Another top favourite among local hikers is Camino de Villaflor — circular by Aveinte, a moderate 6.4 miles (10.4 km) path. This trail offers a circular journey through the countryside near Aveinte, featuring gentle terrain. Local hikers also love the Hiking loop from Fontiveros, a 4.1 miles (6.6 km) trail leading through open rural landscapes, often completed in about 1 hour 40 minutes. Hiking around Crespos is defined by gentle rural paths, historical routes, and open countryside. The network offers options for both easy and moderate ability levels. The guide includes highly-rated routes that showcase the best of the region's landscape. More than 4 hikers have used komoot to explore Crespos's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: Are there easy hiking trails suitable for beginners in Crespos?
A: Yes, Crespos offers several easy trails. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1249233287/castillo-del-duque-de-montellano-circular-desde-munomer-del-peco" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castillo del Duque de Montellano — circular desde Muñomer del Peco&lt;/a&gt; is an easy 3.0-mile (4.9 km) route with minimal elevation gain. Another option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40001982" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hiking loop from Fontiveros&lt;/a&gt;, which is 4.1 miles (6.6 km) and also considered easy.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on hikes around Crespos?
A: The trails around Crespos are characterized by gentle elevation changes and open countryside. You'll often find yourself on historical paths connecting small villages and agricultural landscapes, making for a generally smooth hiking experience.

Q: Are there any circular hiking routes in the Crespos area?
A: Yes, many routes in Crespos are circular, allowing you to start and end in the same location. A notable example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1137629733/camino-de-villaflor-circular-by-aveinte" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Camino de Villaflor — circular by Aveinte&lt;/a&gt;, a moderate 6.4-mile (10.4 km) path that explores the countryside near Aveinte.

Q: What are the typical distances and durations for hikes in Crespos?
A: Hikes in Crespos vary in length, with options ranging from shorter walks like the 3.0-mile (4.9 km) &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1249233287/castillo-del-duque-de-montellano-circular-desde-munomer-del-peco" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castillo del Duque de Montellano — circular desde Muñomer del Peco&lt;/a&gt;, which takes about 1 hour 15 minutes, to longer routes such as the 9.3-mile (15.0 km) &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1146218436/castillo-palacio-de-castronuevo-circular-por-los-parajes-de-la-mora" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castillo-Palacio de Castronuevo — circular por los Parajes de La Mora&lt;/a&gt;, typically completed in under 4 hours.

Q: Are there any historical sites or landmarks to see along the hiking trails?
A: Yes, the region is rich in history. You can explore sites like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5768238" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castronuevo Castle-Palace&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3692892" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ruins of Castronuevo&lt;/a&gt;. Many paths also lead past traditional villages and agricultural structures, offering a glimpse into the local heritage.

Q: Is Crespos a good destination for family-friendly hikes?
A: Given the gentle elevation changes and generally easy to moderate difficulty of the trails, Crespos is well-suited for family outings. Shorter, easy routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1249233287/castillo-del-duque-de-montellano-circular-desde-munomer-del-peco" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castillo del Duque de Montellano — circular desde Muñomer del Peco&lt;/a&gt; are particularly good options for families with children.

Q: What is the best time of year to go hiking in Crespos?
A: The best time to hike in Crespos is generally during spring and autumn when the weather is mild and the landscapes are vibrant. Summer can be warm, so early mornings or late afternoons are preferable. Winter offers a different charm, though some paths might be muddy after rain.

Q: Are dogs allowed on the hiking trails in Crespos?
A: While specific regulations can vary, most rural trails in Crespos are generally dog-friendly, especially if your dog is well-behaved and kept on a leash. Always ensure you carry water for your pet and clean up after them to respect the local environment and other hikers.

Q: Are there any less crowded hiking options in Crespos?
A: The Crespos region is known for its rural character and generally less crowded trails compared to more popular hiking destinations. Most routes offer a peaceful experience through open countryside and small villages, allowing for a tranquil escape.

Q: What should I bring for a hike in Crespos?
A: For hiking in Crespos, it's advisable to bring comfortable walking shoes, water, sun protection (hat, sunscreen), and appropriate clothing layers for changing weather. A map or a GPS device like komoot is also recommended, as some paths might be less marked.

Q: Are there places to eat or drink near the hiking routes in Crespos?
A: Many hiking routes in Crespos pass through or near small villages. While not every village will have a dedicated cafe, you can often find local establishments or small shops where you can purchase refreshments or enjoy a meal after your hike, especially in larger villages like Fontiveros.

Q: What kind of wildlife might I encounter on the trails?
A: The agricultural and open countryside around Crespos is home to various bird species, small mammals, and insects. Keep an eye out for local birds of prey soaring overhead or common field animals. Maintaining a respectful distance and quiet observation will enhance your chances of spotting wildlife.

Q: Are there any viewpoints or scenic spots along the trails?
A: The open landscapes of Crespos naturally offer expansive views of the Castilian countryside. While there aren't specific 'viewpoint' highlights in the traditional sense, the gentle elevations provide continuous panoramic vistas of fields, distant villages, and historical structures like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3704019" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castle of Narros de Saldueña&lt;/a&gt;.
tips: In what is called Las Capellanas we find the remains of the church in Castronuevo, close to a small pond that is there and where from afar, you can see the castle of Castronuevo.

The Castle-Palace of Castronuevo, also known as Castle of Rivilla de Barajas, is an impressive fortification located in the municipality of Rivilla de Barajas, in the province of Ávila, within the autonomous community of Castilla y León. Although its current use is null, its history and structure reveal its former greatness.
It was erected by Gil de Vivero before 1481, on the site of Castronuevo, and later remodeled by his son before selling it in 1489 to the Duke of Alba. The castle has three circular towers and two rectangular towers.
Despite its age and danger of collapse, this castle remains a fascinating testament to medieval history and architecture. 🏰🌟
In this flat area which is called La Mora and which is located between small border towns between the provinces of Salamanca and Ávila, where cultivated fields are combined with open oak forests.
